clean_story:
Moon’s pull. Body clocks tuned.

"Tide-predicting" animals don’t use calendars—they feel the ocean’s rhythm. Their bodies act like living tide charts, syncing to lunar cycles and water pressure.

Grunion fish ride waves ashore only during spring tides’ highest surges, timed to the moon’s full/new phases. Their eggs hatch 10 days later, when the next high tide returns—no parent needed. Crabs like Uca pugilator burrow deeper as water rises, sensing vibrations through leg hairs finer than spider silk. Even coral spawns in mass synchronized bursts, releasing eggs on nights after a full moon, when tides are strongest.

The mechanism? A mix of circatidal clocks (24.8-hour body rhythms, matching the moon’s orbit) and pressure-sensitive cells. Some species, like the mangrove killifish, survive in drying pools by slowing their metabolism to a near-stop—waiting for the tide’s return like a paused video game.

Three related subjects are moon-driven animal behaviors, biological clocks in extremes, and how humans mimic nature’s timing.
